speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred Winter, July 11, 1996, in Norwalk, Connecticut. Language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about some of the sabotage in the PX. Can you think of other instances where you received supplies and did not distribute them to the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We stole whole cases of cigarettes also-- 25,000 cigarettes in every case-- and gave some to the Latvians for things in return. Also, we had the taste for cognac. And one of the Latvian Jews know how to open a bottle without damaging the cork. So we were making the bottle half empty--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you-- you told us that on the last tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about the meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The meat. Oh, yeah. The meat-- somebody had to go to [NON-ENGLISH], where the slaughterhouse was, to pick up the meat for the SS, and the sausage, and everything for the concentration camp. Now, Jews would get only horse meat and sausage from horse meat, while the SS would get everything beef. But it was in the same cases, only the cases had labels, saying horse meat or beef. So while they were transporting the beef to Dondangen, he would switch the labels. The SS would have the horse meat, the sausage from the horse meat. And the Jews would have the salami from the beef. The same we did with butter. We've explained that it's not enough butter for regiment to move out. So the regiment would move with margarine, and the concentration camp would get the butter afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you planned these activities at night in the PX, how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt great. Also, we knew what was going on on the front, because they had a radio in the PX. And the Germans turn on the radio, and they left sometimes. And we played around with the radio and listened to the radio free Europe or Russia, or whatever it was, which was there. And we know where the front lines were. We heard the day when the invasion in France was, and everything we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay at this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till July, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you told that you were going to be moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning when we were going to work, we got told, nobody goes. But two of the Latvian Jews were missing already. And I found out later on, they overheard a phone call that the-- that the Jews will be taken out. All the Jews from Dondangen will be removed, because the Russians come near. And they two escaped to a Latvian farmer, whom they had supplied with cigarettes and tobacco, which we had stolen before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you told you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told we are going to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we had to march towards Libau. We marched only for three or four days. At nights, we were out in the field. And the first night, quite a few Latvian Jews escaped. And they took, although, a number of guards along, which were German or Lithuanian guards. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the second day, some tried to escape in daytime, and they got shot. Then the Hungarian women were marching in front of us. There were 800 girls from Munkacs. And we had heard from them what Auschwitz was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew by this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, we know what Auschwitz was. They told us about the gas chambers and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. So Dondangen-- because we gave the food to the camp and everything, we had to feed all the regiment-- had 3,800 Jewish prisoners of that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, officially, all 3,800. How many escaped, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the march take?

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched three days. And on the fourth day, we were put on a train to Libau, Liepaja in Latvian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were guards, who said, anyone who goes-- goes over the side gets shot. Otherwise, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they cattle cars or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Open boxcars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Open boxcars. And how long did it take you for the rest of the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three or four hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in Libau. And we were put in a farm or leather factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first two days, we didn't do anything. And there was no food. On the third day we got food. One of the Latvian Jews put his head over the wall to talk to somebody else, and the guards shot him. And he was only wounded. And [NON-ENGLISH] came out and told him he is not getting any dressing, nothing. And he let him lie on the ground to bleed to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came and told him that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The chief medical officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were? At this point, did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because a day later, 25 Jews were needed to work in the harbor. And one Latvian Jew spoke fluently German with a German accent. And he said he was from Cassel. So he went with us. And I was one of the 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, you were selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to work in the harbor, loading ships.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no camp nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were nothing. The ghetto was obliterated in '43. And we worked only a few days in the harbor. And we got enough food to eat there. And we found out how far the Russians were advanced and that they were carrying off everything, that they were close to Riga. They would be in Riga soon. And we found out quite a few things what we didn't know before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: How far the Americans had advanced into France already and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay here in the harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked only four or five days there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put in boxcars to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, concentration camp, near Gdansk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first feeling when you saw Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were came out of the boxcar, SS was standing there with dogs and rifles. And we had to line up, and they marched us in the camp. And one SS man told us, this is, see, wire you cannot cross. If you cross the wire, you will be shot. On further is an electrified wire. You'll be electrocuted. You are in isolation here in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still were with the 25 young men from the PX before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there-- there were nearly half. Nearly all the Latvian Jews had escaped [INAUDIBLE] and one of the women with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your numbers were smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The women were separated from the men in, in Stutthof. But one thing we saw in Stutthof, which we didn't like, a crematorium. And that was smoking day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the first one you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how your heart was, what was happening in your mind when you saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- there was no picnic in this camp, because when we got the food rations, it was only-- we saw right away that was, something was missing on our food. And a lot of beating was going on from the fellows who were giving out the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I only know the first two letters-- 57,000, something with that. I don't know the rest anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a pal in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had role call twice a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job while you were at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They had no work for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could lie down on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not-- unfinished building, where we could go when it was raining. Otherwise, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay here?

speaker: interviewee
text: A good three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your other observations of Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: That a lot of abuse was going on by from other fellow prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say specifically who was against who?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of criminals and a lot of Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the criminals from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some German, some Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had a green triangle on their clothing. This was criminal. And the Poles had a p in it. And some with a red triangle with a p were also [INAUDIBLE], political Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Poles and the political prisoners against you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If they were Poles, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone personally try to hurt you or argue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not make contact with anyone over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a-- with a certain group, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your group? Then how were you told that you were going to go someplace else?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- we had a role call. And the German SS officer came out and said, I need 1,000 volunteers to go to Buchenwald. And I know what Buchenwald camp was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because a lot of Jews from the Rhine district were sent to Buchenwald in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we knew there was also a lot of abuse. And we knew that Buchenwald was run by political prisoners. And we said, so we have a better chance in Buchenwald to survive than here with the Poles. Let's go to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: By freight car. First, we went into showers. We were stripped, got other prison uniforms. The only thing we could keep were our shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number once you were taken to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stutthof, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 57,000 something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there another selection process once you got to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no selection process in Buchenwald. In Buchenwald, we were stripped and put in the showers again. And we could pick up our shoes later on. And that was important for me to get my shoes back, because I had pictures hidden in the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pictures of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how long were you at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe four weeks. We were-- we worked there. We went in this little camp, what they called the small camp, where all the newcomers came in. And there was a tent. And otherwise, we were sleeping outside. In the tent, you couldn't go, because the fleas would eat you up. Another, elsewhere were some straw bags outside. You could lie down on [? them ?]. Once in awhile, they would march us to the quarry to pick up stones. Otherwise, nothing. And this-- we were there maybe a week or 10 days. Then Poles got shipped in, 1,000 Poles. And it came to a big battle between the Jews and the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the battle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Poles wanted the straw bags, we Jews had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they want-- to sleep on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we said no. And the big fight started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, out. There were no barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside. And then the camp police came in. They were mostly Czechs. When the Poles got a beating, I never have seen something like that in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Czechs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then the Poles got lined up and got told, here are no Jews, no Catholics, no Protestants, and no Poles. There's nothing here except prisoners. And every prisoner is alike. And if you have different ideas, turn around. There's a crematorium there for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. How did that speech impress you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were flabbergasted. The first time in all those years that somebody was standing up for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between three and four weeks in that little camp. Only when it wasn't-- during that time, one day we had visitors from the United States Air Force.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 27 minutes, the day turned into night. And it was an earthquake. The entire factories around Buchenwald, where prisoners worked-- [NON-ENGLISH] was that. They were building war material. And the entire buildings where the SS have housing, and administration offices, and everything got flattened out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who flattened them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American bombers. And only two or three bombs fell in the camps. They tried to hit the crematorium, but they missed and hit surrounding area. As far as can recall, from the records, what I heard, close to 800 prisoners got killed during that air raid. And I don't know how many from the SS. We didn't get the numbers, but we know that quite a few SS got also killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days later, we were chased in the showers again and got-- first, we had to shave. We had to put shaving cream on our faces. And Then. We got shaved in the showers. Got new prison clothes. Got all a number to sew on. My number was 83279. We could keep our shoes when we went in the showers. And then we had to march in what was the movie house actually, originally. Pictures taken, and fingerprints taken so far. But anyone who had a picture taken, the minute the flash went on, had to jump off that chair. If not, he had a needle in his thigh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get him off the chair.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life continue there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would-- we were surprised how much food we were getting compared to Stutthof, because everyone was getting the same ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were not working?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the next move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we got selected to [NON-ENGLISH] commander [NON-ENGLISH]. They were making gasoline from coal. And there were 1,000 Jews were selected to be shipped the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you shipped to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Troglitz. That's in Saxonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is a state in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. Not far from [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a factory, which had been bombed by the Allies, which were making gasoline, and had to be rebuilt by concentration camp prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my job. But when we arrived, the camps were only big tents with barbed wire around. And there were 2,000 Jews via Auschwitz that had come from Munkacs. Now, the Hungarian Jews were down to bottom line. There was no life in them. The only thing what was in them was religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a lot of religious observances?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come to that now. [INAUDIBLE] The Hungarian Jews, they were praying all day if they could, because they were very orthodox. And they had no resistance. They didn't do anything. We, the Jews who came from the ghetto, we were fighting back right away. We couldn't do much. I was assigned to build gunner placements. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were doing that-- were you doing that for the anti-aircraft?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the anti-aircraft guns. And we were getting cigarettes in the camp. But I wasn't smoking. And one German-- I spoke the same dialect as he did-- asked me from where do I come. And when I told him from where, he said, I come from next door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he asked me, you got cigarettes? I said, yeah, I got 10 cigarettes. Let me have some cigarettes. I give you a whole loaf of bread for it. This is a Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a German soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: German soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did. When I came in the camp, I traded a half a bread for 10 cigarettes. So the next day, I told him, I got another 10 cigarettes. You have another loaf of bread? This way, I had plenty of bread to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Did you have another job there in that camp also?

speaker: interviewee
text: After maybe four or six weeks, I went to the delousing, which we could go any time you could swindle your way in. You didn't work that day. And when I'm lying in the evening in the tent, we had no blankets. We had body bags we slept in. My numbers being called up. And one of the fellow next to me said, you better give me your shoes. You're in trouble. I said, wait a minute. You can't have my shoes yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was your number being called?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a friend. He was a kapo in the kitchen, a potato peelers. And he promised me when it gets cold, he gets me a job in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my number got called up. And I'm going. There I see, what they call, the report fuhrer,-- that is the second in command of the camp-- standing there with a kapo and another fellow I didn't know. Your name, so and so? Yep. You know arithmetic? Yeah. So they asked me a few questions. And I answered them right away back. You come with us. So I went with them. You sleep over here from now on. You're going to work in the food magazine. You figure out the daily rations. Oh. Like you did at another camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So in a food magazine, the SS kitchen and the camp kitchen was the same at that time. But the SS got sausage, came in metal containers. And you could kill somebody with those salamis, that hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to a fellow, we could have some salami too. He said, there's no chance. You have to weigh it out, and there's nothing left over. I told him, you ever heard of putting a couple of pails of water in it? Overnight, the salami will absorb all the water on [INAUDIBLE]. So you got your salami?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I got our first salami. And then later on, they moved the kitchen out. And they built a new camp in [NON-ENGLISH]. We moved in that kitchen also, a new kitchen and everything. So we had to steal from-- sorry to say-- from other prisoners to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were with the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish prisoners, and Gentile also later on. And also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the kapos, which we are all Gentile, had special food, better than what the Jews had. So we were stealing from the kapos also. Though I worked in food magazine, I was the only German Jew in the food magazine. The rest were Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got along fine. But only one thing was bad. And that happened before I came in the kitchen. It was the day of Yom Kippur, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness what happened on Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Hungarian Jews went to the SS and told the SS, tomorrow is Yom Kippur, the day of fast. Will you shut down the water and not give out any food for 24 hours so that we all can celebrate the day of Yom Kippur? And we guarantee you that the work performance will not suffer. And the SS took that with a smile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No food was given out for 24 hours. No water to wash or to drink either. But the work performance had not to suffer. And how many bodies and unconscious got carried in the camp afterwards, I don't know. It was a catastrophic thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a slaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not a slaughter. But it was terrible. And we all, all the German Jews, said, those orthodox should go six feet under. They should never have a chance to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you saw on that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were-- the Germans were pushing on us more than anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for work production?

speaker: interviewee
text: For work production. But you've had no water to drink, and you had no food for 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month is it now, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: July. No, you mean in the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of September. September, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of September, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that camp in [NON-ENGLISH], Theresienstadt, till April, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in April of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: The front was coming closer. The Americans were coming. But in the meantime, we had air attacks day and night. In the daytime, the Americans. At nighttime, the British.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how you felt hearing those attacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful. Because we couldn't work. I mean, we in the kitchen had to work. But the other prisoners could not work. And they bombed the railroad tracks, which were close to the camp. And one bomb cart came in the camp. And one prisoner-- it was an [INAUDIBLE] from Cologne-- got killed. And we were happy. And one day, they bombed the town next to it. And we had to go out for rescue from the camp. But they had time-delayed bombs there, which started to explode. And they marched us back in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I-- before we marched in, I liberated a goose. Where did you liberate the goose from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From a German. And I twisted the neck and carried the goose into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with the goose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I plucked the feathers, and we cooked it and had a good dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Another, another fellow bought in a rabbit. And we had some cats in the camp. So we killed a cat. We had the rabbit. [LAUGHS] And the fellow got the cat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make a stew?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the cat [LAUGHS] for the other fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Germans around you acting at this time with all the bombings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were fair. Some were very anti-Semitic. And the camp commander was a bastard. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was one ethnic German from Romania. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He did a lot of beating. He knocked my tooth out.

speaker: interviewer
text: He knocked your tooth out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I run out of the kitchen. I had diarrhea. And I bumped into him. He caught me begging. And he hit me with a pistol butt in the mouth, and it broke a tooth.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans around you, hearing all these attacks by the Americans, did they-- what did they decide to--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the civilian, we had no contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But I mean, what did-- what did they decide to do with you at this point? Nothing. Work, work, work. Try to rebuild. In April of 1945, what did they decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep the Jews on working. There is a factory going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the next action?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the front came closer, the next action was to take us away. And it was decided to take us to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could this be called the [INAUDIBLE] march?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, yes, because there were over 1,500 prisoners left in the camp. They had sent out a couple of thousand, which were sick, prior to that to Bergen-Belsen. And from those who were sent to Bergen-Belsen, there is no survivor. Because I tried to trace them after the war, and there is no survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did this march look like that started in April of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put in boxcars, 50 to a boxcar. And we were sent via Dresden into the [NON-ENGLISH] Mountain. We came to a stop. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And we had to dig a grave for those who had died already. And-- [? while we ?] after we had buried those, we got back in the train. They gave us some food. And then the train moved out with two engines. And then we were maybe a few miles away, the train got attacked by American fighter planes.
